title: DC30 Labs 2–4: Buffer Overflow on MVS
description: Overflow the HELLO program's 150-byte gets() buffer, find the return address in a dump, use De Bruijn patterns to pinpoint the offset, and control execution to print a WTO message. Classic stack overflow — on a mainframe from 1978.

steps.title: The Vulnerable Program
steps.expected: Understand why gets() is dangerous and how a stack overflow works.
steps.hint: gets() is banned in modern C (removed in C11). On MVS/370 the stack is in the same address space as program code — overflowing it overwrites the return address stored by STM (Store Multiple Registers).

steps.title: Lab 2: View the Overflow File in Hex
steps.screen:
ISPF EDIT - HERC01.OVERFLOW(LGBT400)
 Command ===> HEX ON

 000001 D3C7C2E3D3C7C2E3D3C7C2E3D3C7C2E3  LGTBLGTBLGTBLGTB
        D3C7C2E3D3C7C2E3D3C7C2E3D3C7C2E3
 000002 D3C7C2E3D3C7C2E3D3C7C2E3D3C7C2E3  LGTBLGTBLGTBLGTB
        ...

 This record is 400 bytes wide — 250 bytes past the buffer.
 LGTB in EBCDIC = D3 C7 C2 E3

 Submit LAB02 — it will ABEND with SYSTEM 0C6 (data exception)
steps.expected: See the overflow input in hex. Submit LAB02 and get an 0C6 abend.
steps.hint: 0C6 = Data Exception abend. The program tried to execute our overflow data as an instruction. That's good — it means we overwrote the return address. Now we need to find WHERE in the 400-byte buffer the return address sits.

steps.title: Lab 2: Read the Dump
steps.screen:
ISPF BROWSE - HERC01.DUMP001
 Command ===> F D3C7C2E3

 0B40AC  D3C7C2E3 D3C7C2E3 D3C7C2E3 D3C7C2E3  LGTBLGTBLGTBLGTB
 0B40BC  D3C7C2E3 D3C7C2E3 D3C7C2E3 D3C7C2E3  LGTBLGTBLGTBLGTB

 The LGTB pattern starts at address 0B40AC.

 Registers at time of abend (look for RG in dump):
   R14 (return address) = D3C7C2E3  ← overwritten with LGTB!

 This tells us: the return address is somewhere in our buffer.
steps.expected: Find the LGTB pattern in the dump and note the memory address (e.g. 0B40AC).
steps.hint: Use F D3C7C2E3 to search for LGTB in EBCDIC hex. The address in the left column is where our buffer starts in memory. R14 holds the return address — if it shows D3C7C2E3 we've confirmed the overflow.

steps.title: Lab 3: De Bruijn Pattern — Find the Exact Offset
steps.screen:
TSO READY
 CALL 'HERC01.LOAD(DEBRUIJN)' '400'

 AABACADAEAFAGAHAIAJAKALAMANAOA...

 A De Bruijn sequence has the property that every
 N-byte subsequence appears exactly once.

 Feed this to HELLO instead of LGTB:
  → The return address will contain a unique 4-byte pattern
  → Look up that pattern → know the exact byte offset

 Edit HERC01.JCLLIB(LAB03) to use DEBRUIJN output as STDIN
 Submit → get the abend → read R14 from the dump
 Look up the 4 bytes in the De Bruijn sequence → offset found
steps.expected: Run DEBRUIJN REXX, feed output to HELLO via LAB03 JCL, find the exact offset of the return address.
steps.hint: The DEBRUIJN.rex script is in HERC01.EXEC (or SYS2.EXEC). Run it with the length of your overflow (400). The 4 bytes in R14 after the abend tell you exactly which byte in the buffer overwrites the return address.

steps.title: Lab 4: Control Execution — WTO Shellcode
steps.screen:
ISPF EDIT - HERC01.OVERFLOW(WTO400)
 Command ===> HEX ON

 Columns 257-328 contain shellcode instead of LGTB:
  47F0xxxx  BC 15,<address>   Branch to shellcode
  ...
  WTO 'H4CK3D TH3 M41NFR4M3'  ← our payload

 Submit HERC01.JCLLIB(LAB04)

 JES2 JOBLOG output:
  +H4CK3D TH3 M41NFR4M3

 MAX COND CODE 0000
steps.expected: Submit LAB04 and see your WTO message in the job log.
steps.hint: WTO (Write To Operator) is an MVS macro that writes a message to the system console and job log. Our shellcode just calls WTO — proof of code execution. The + prefix in the job log means it's a WTO message.

steps.title: What Just Happened
steps.screen:
Buffer Overflow Summary — MVS Edition

1. HELLO has a 150-byte stack buffer
2. gets() reads 400 bytes from STDIN (our overflow file)
3. Bytes 1-150: fill the buffer
4. Bytes 151-256: overwrite saved registers on the stack
5. Bytes 257-260: overwrite R14 (return address)
6. We set R14 to point to our shellcode (bytes 261+)
7. When main() returns: BR 14 → jumps to our code
8. Our code executes WTO → message in job log

Key differences from x86:
  - No NX/DEP, no ASLR, no stack canaries on MVS 3.8j
  - EBCDIC encoding — shellcode bytes must be valid EBCDIC
  - Return via BR 14 (not RET instruction)
  - Registers saved/restored with STM/LM macros
steps.expected: Understand the complete overflow chain on MVS.
steps.hint: Modern z/OS has ASLR and execute protection, but many legacy applications still use gets() or equivalent. The EBCDIC encoding constraint makes shellcode harder — that's what the e2alookup.py and findbytes.py tools are for.
